title: 第 54 節
en: Dialectic Varieties of Mythological Names.

But there is still stronger evidence to show that these mythological names are not subject to the same stringent phonetic rules as ordinary words. We find that several of these gods and heroes have two or three different names which it would be quite impossible to explain by the ordinary phonetic rules of Greek, but which have to be accepted as PanAryan varieties. In the case of UocretScov, for instance, we know that he was also called Horn. UocreiSacov, Ark. Hoo-oiSdv, Lak. UoolSdp, Boeot. IToreiSawv, and ITotoiScuy/>9 1, also HocreLSrjs, LTocrtS^?, and IToTtSas 2. Now suppose that some etymology or other had been satisfactorily established for Poseidon, and there is at least a plausible form 77-/509 + oiSaw, to swell toward, how could we exriect the same etymology to account for Potidas ? And yet we can hardly doubt that all these names are dialectic varieties of one and the same typical form. They may with some effort be accounted for by means of the phonetic laws of each special Greek dialect, but they could not be brought into harmony with the phonetic laws that determine the correspondence of Sanskrit and Greek consonants and 1 Brugmann, V. G., i, 490, 2 ; cf. Curtius, p. 245, who quotes Fick, i3, 507, from eido, to swell ; cf. Prellwitz, Diet., s. v.
2 Gerhard, Gr. Myth, 231,1.
400 VARIETIES OF MYTHOLOGICAL NAMES. [chap.

vowels. If the first element corresponds to the Sk. preposition prati, the Greek npoTt, this Trport would have suffered changes which have nothing parallel to them in ordinary Greek. According to Baunack we should have to admit nine varieties in Greek, irpori, nopTL, irepTi, irpos, nori, ttot, tto, 7705, and iroi This is a large allowance, and the question would still have to be left open, whether we have to admit two parallel forms from the beginning, or take the forms without p as modifications of the forms with p. The Persian forms patiy and paiti favour the former view, but we are driven again to take refuge in Ur-indogermanisch in order to give phonetic reasons for what in each special language would be simply impossible.
Other divine names in Greek which offer perplexing, though probably dialectic, varieties are :

\0rjvr], Adr\vair\, ' 'A6r)va, ' ' Addva, *A6ava : Tplrco, TplraivLS, Tpiraia, TpiToyeveia, Tpiro/xi^is : Ep/i^?, Epjieias, Ep/xao? : Ecrrta, 'Icttlt] : 'AttoXXojv, 'AtteXXojp, ATreiko)v, AttXovv. Aprjs and "Apevs: 'A^poSirr), ' Atypoyiveia and Acf)pa> : ArjjjLyJTrjp and A^w : Ba/r^o?, 'iaK^os: 'AlSt^?, C1S77S, vAig: Aio^ucro?, Atw^vcro?. In some cases these varieties can be accounted for by general phonetic rules ; in others, however, even when the difference is only one between a long and a short vowel, they would upset any etymology, however carefully elaborated. And what applies to Greek mythological names applies likewise to the names of ancient or modern German mythology. If at the present moment, or at all events during the present century, a scholar were to collect the legends about the goddess Holda or Frau Holda, he would hear her name pronounced as Frau Holle, Frau Wolle, vj VARIETIES OF MYTHOLOGICAL NAMES. 40I or Frau Eolle, without any respect for phonetic rules ; just as even now we can hear St. Bartholomew pronounced Bartlemy, Barklemy, or Bardlemy1.

All this will help us to understand why phonetic rules which may admit of no exception as between Sanskrit and Greek, must necessarily be modified so as to include the changes which are recognised in the local dialects of every language.
Aspirates, Sonant and Surd.

Nothing, for instance, can be more certain than that, as a rule, a Sanskrit or a Proto- Aryan media ought not in Greek to appear as a tenuis. But it is equally certain that if an Aryan word has once assumed its Greek garb, it is liable to any amount of dialectical change. In Greek itself we find ever so many instances of a tenuis instead of a media, not only in the middle, but also at the beginning of a word. We have not only "Kpre^ir by the side of "A/ore/xiS, ©e/xtr by the side of ®e/xiS, or aprjya) by the side of dpKeo), Ttjyavov by the side of rrJKO), Kdvcofios for KdVco7ro5, but we find Sct7rt? instead of T<x7ri9 or TOLTrr]<;, yva/xi//cu for kW/xi/zcu, yvafjxxWov for Kvefyak\ov, fiaTelv for Trareiv, /3iKpo<? for ttik/oos. Even with aspirated tenuis we find dialectically media for tenuis aspirata, as in B1A177-77-0S for <E>i\i7r7ro9, BepevUr) for QepeviKT).
Such changes, therefore, though exceptional or dialectic only, cannot be deprecated on principle, if they occur in mythological names.
No one has ever doubted that the Greek 6 is 1 Dickens, Martin Chuzzlewit, p. 558. VOL. I. D d 432 ASPIRATES, SONANT AND SURD. [chap.

an aspirated tenuis and not an aspirated media, and therefore phonetically quite distinct from Sk. dh. Nevertheless Greek 6 corresponds regularly to Sk. aspirated media dh, which Greek does no longer possess, such as in dvfxos = Sk. dhuma, &c. This Greek 0 is reduplicated with t, nTfy/u, while dh in Sanskrit is reduplicated with d, da-dhami. Grassmann, however, in his essay published in 1863, showed that there are roots which require the admission of an aspirate both as initial and as final, such as DHIGH, in Sk. DIH ; and that in Greek, which has no sonant aspirates, this root would appear as either tlx or Giy, and not as St^ or dhiy.

Why this should have been so, why what is media aspirata in Sanskrit, should, when losing its aspiration, be represented in Greek by a tenuis, is difficult to explain with certainty. It may have been due either to the reaction of the final aspirate, which in Greek could be tenuis only, or to the fact that the initial aspirate was at first tenuis aspirata, and therefore its locum tenens a tenuis also.

There may have been, or, as some people might say, there must have been an intermediate stage in which the initial media had not yet become a tenuis, that is to say the root might have been Six before it became tlx, though of this there is no trace in the actual language. We find derivatives such as rolxos and Oiyydvco, but never any derivatives from hix *• This Otyyava), aor. ediyov and Oiyeiv, is quite correct phonetically, yet such was formerly the respect shown for the meaning of words that 1 Science of Language, ii, p. 269.

v] ASPIRATES, SONANT AND SURD. 403 Grassmann himself actually hesitated * to trace Ouyeiv back to the root DHIGH, simply on account of the difference of meaning. Yet the original sense of digh, to knead, might well, as in mm, have been taken in the more general sense of handling, touching, considering. The same concessions, however, should sometimes be made by semasiologists which have so often to be made by the phonologists.
Mythological Names Prehistoric.

That the Greek language passed through a period of uncertainty as to the best way of representing the sound which in Sanskrit appears as media aspirata, gh, dh, bh, we know from the numerous instances in which in certain roots media and tenuis vary2. The very fact that the final aspirate of roots like DHIGH is represented by media as in diyeiv, not by tenuis (6lk), shows that its medial character continued to be felt. If then the medial sound, peculiar to the period of transition, was regularly preserved in the final of this class of roots, why should it not formerly 3 have been preserved in the initial also ?
Daphne.
If dih leads to toI-)(o<;, why should not Daphne stand for Taphne, a form phonetically more correct, 1 K. Z., xii, p. 125.
2 On aTOfifpoi and a-repPa, &c, see Science of Language, ii, p. 270.

3 Grassmann speaks of the transition of the initial media into a tenuis aspirata as a chronological event, K. Z., xii, p. 117: ' Da die Aspirate im Sanskrit weich, im Griechischen, wenigstens von einem gewissen Zeitpunkte an, hart war.'
D d 2 404 DAPHNE. [CHAP.

though extinct in classical Greek? Whenever we try to discover the etymology and the original intention of a mythological name, it seems to me that the first we have to do is to ascertain the class of physical phenomena to which certain names point. In the case of Daphne, I think I have proved that the story of her fleeing before Phoibos (the brilliant sun) and vanishing in his embraces, can be matched by other cognate stories, all pointing to the Dawn as vanishing as soon as the brilliant rays of the rising sun touch her. We have therefore a perfect right to expect in Daphne a name of the Dawn. Then, and then only, comes the question, how the Dawn could be called by such a name as Daphne ? That such a name had an etymology, that it was formed with a purpose no true scholar would deny. Let therefore any one produce a better etymology than that which I suggested many years ago, from DAH {Bay, Tax : Ool/3, ra(f> : Sacft), and my own will, of course, have to be surrendered. But it must not be forgotten that my etymology explains not only Daphne as a name of the Dawn, but likewise daphne as a name of the laurel-tree into which she was fabled to have been changed \ I have always been ready to give up any etymology, provided always that it could be replaced by a better one ; but when I consider the fluctuating state of tenuis aspirata and media in Greek (Brugmann, Grundriss, par. 469, 8), when I see actual dialectic changes such as Sct7ri9 for rd-n-qs in the language of Aristophanes and Xenophon, I maintain that until a better etymology, better not only phonetically but 1 See Science of Language, ii, p. 621.
v] DAPHNE. 405 materially also, can be produced, my own still holds the field.

It is possible to account for the initial media in $d<f)i>7] instead of tol^vt] even on more general grounds. What Grassmann has shown (K. Z., xii, p. no) is that when roots having originally an initial and final aspirate, appear in Greek, the initial, deprived of its aspiration, appears as tenuis, the final as aspirate. But if a word was formed before the Aryan languages separated, such a word would hardly have been liable to a rule which is exclusively Greek. Now most mythological names belong to what may be called a prehistoric or pre-ethnic stratum of Aryan speech, and a name such as Dahana would appear as Daphana or Daphna, just as regularly as garbha appears as fipefos, and as SeX^u? also as Se\cf)6s in a-$e\<j)6$. The root of garbha is gribh or gn'h (according to Grassmann, ghrabh). In a similar way we have /3v#/ao? by the side of nvO/x-qp, fiaTTTO) by the side of gahate, and fiadvs, fiados, /3ev0os, whether we connect them with gah (ghah), or, according to Fick, with bhadh ; we have So\t^o<? by the side of dirgha or *daregha, &c.
Athene.

From the same root DAH, or rather from its twin form AH, as seen in ahan, day, I ventured many years ago to derive the name of Athene, the day -goddess, the goddess of light and wisdom. Many objections were raised, and I have tried to answer them all in my Science of Language, ii, p. 621 seq. As in this case, however, another and a very plausible etymology had been proposed, namely by Professor Benfey, I felt bound to show 406 ATHENE. [CHAP.

why his derivation was phonetically, as well as materially, objectionable 1. Whenever there is a choice between two etymologies, it becomes necessary that we should show the grounds on which either the one or the other must be rejected.

It was not a new objection that has lately been raised, namely that if Ahana is connected with ahan, day, its h requires ^ in Greek, and not 6. But I thought I had fully shown on former occasions that with regard to the Sanskrit aspirated media (i. e. Greek aspirated tenuis), there was a time of which the clearest traces are left in Sanskrit, when the h was as yet undetermined locally, and found expression freely either as gh, or dh, or bh. Thus besides nah, we find nabh (nabhi) and nadh (naddha). Besides grah, we find grabh and gradh (gWdhra). Besides gah (gac?Aa, deep) we find gabh (gabhira, deep) and gadh (gadha, ford). In cases like these we ask no longer whether the final h or gh was palatal or velar, or labial or dental, but we take such a root as gadh as an independent type, and derive from it fiaOvs, deep, while we derive fiaTTTa) from gabh (gabh).
zh: 神話名稱的辯證變體

但還有更強有力的證據表明，這些神話名稱並不受制於與普通詞彙相同的嚴格音韻規則。我們發現，這些神祇與英雄中有數個具有兩個或三個不同的名稱，用普通的古希臘語音韻規則是完全無法解釋的，但卻必須被視為泛雅利安的變體。以 Ποσειδῶν 為例，我們知道他也被稱為 Ποσ-, Ποσειδάων（Ark. Πο-οιδάν, Lak. Ποσιδάν, Boeot. Ποτειδάων 及 Ποτοιδάων）¹，以及 Ποσειδῆς、Ποσειδῆος 及 Ποτιδᾶς²。現在假設某種詞源學已經為波塞冬（Poseidon）成功建立起來，至少存在一個似是而非的形式 π- /σύος＋οἰδάω「膨脹而起」（to swell toward），我們又怎能期待同樣的詞源學來說明 Potidas 呢？然而我們幾乎無法懷疑，所有這些名稱都是同一個典型形式的方言變體。它們雖可以費力地用各自古希臘方言的音韻規律加以說明，但卻無法與決定梵語與古希臘語輔音對應關係的音韻規律協調一致， ¹ Brugmann, V. G., i, 490, 2; cf. Curtius, p. 245, 引自 Fick, i³, 507，源自 eido「膨脹」；cf. Prellwitz, Diet., s. v.
2 Gerhard, Gr. Myth, 231,1.
四百 神話名稱的變異 [章節

元音。如果第一個成分對應於梵文前置詞 prati、希臘文 προτι，那麼這個 προτι 會經歷一些在普通希臘文中完全沒有平行例證的變化。按照包納克（Baunack）的說法，我們應該承認希臘文中有九種變異：προτι、ποτι、περτι、προς、ποτ、ποι、πο、πος 和 ποι。這是一個很大的讓步，而問題仍然懸而未決，即我們是否必須承認從一開始就存在兩種平行的形式，還是應該將沒有 p 的形式視為帶有 p 的形式的變化。波斯語形式 patiy 和 paiti 支持前一種觀點，但我們再次被迫求助於原始印歐語（Ur-indogermanisch），以便為那些在每一種特殊語言中都根本不可能的現象提供音韻學上的理由。
其他在希臘文中呈現令人困惑的（雖然可能屬於方言）變體的神聖名稱如下：

在某些情況下，這些變異可以用一般語音規則來解釋；但在其他情況下，即使差異僅在於長母音與短母音之間，這些變異也會破壞任何再精心推敲的詞源學。同樣適用於希臘神話名稱的規律，也適用於古代或現代德國神話中的名稱。假如現在，或至少在當今世紀，有一位學者著手蒐集關於女神荷爾達（Holda）或芙勞‧荷爾達（Frau Holda）的傳說，他會聽到她的名字被念成芙勞‧霍勒（Frau Holle）、芙勞‧沃勒（Frau Wolle）或芙勞‧埃歐勒（Frau Eolle），毫不顧及語音規則；正如即使在今天，我們仍可聽到聖巴爾多祿茂（St. Bartholomew）被念成巴特勒米（Bartlemy）、巴克勒米（Barklemy）或巴德勒米（Bardlemy）。
所有這些將有助於我們理解，為什麼在梵文與希臘文之間可能不容有任何例外的音韻規則，在涵及每種語言的各地方言中所公認的變化時，必然需要經過修正。
送氣音、濁音與清音。

沒有什麼比以下事實更確定了：作為一般規則，梵文或原始雅利安的濁音在希臘文中本不應該出現為清音。然而，同樣確定的是，一旦一個雅利安詞彙一經採納了希臘語的形式，它就可能經歷任何程度的方言變化。在希臘文本身中，我們發現許多清音替代濁音的例子，不僅出現在詞彙的中間，也出現在詞首。我們不僅看到「Κρέμις」與「Λόρευκις」並存、「Θέμις」與「Θέμις」並存，或「ἀρήγω」與「ἀρκέω」並存、「τήγανον」與「τήκω」並存、「Κάνωμος」用於「Κάνωπος」，而且我們發現「σαπτύς」代替「τάπτις」或「τάπτης」、「γναμψικαι」代替「κνάμψικαι」、「γναμψαλλον」代替「Κνέφαλλον」、「μαπείν」代替「πατείν」、「βίκρος」代替「πίκρος」。甚至對於有氣清音，我們也發現方言上以濁音代替有氣清音，如「Φιλίππος」作為「Βιλίππος」，「Θερενίκη」作為「Βερενίκη」。
因此，儘管這類變化僅屬例外性或方言性質，但若其出現在神話名稱中，便不應原則上加以貶低。
無人曾懷疑希臘語 6 是 1¹ --- ¹ Dickens, Martin Chuzzlewit, p. 558.

一個清音送氣音而非濁音送氣音，因此音韻上與梵文 dh 截然不同。然而希臘文 θ 規則地對應於梵文的濁音送氣音 dh，而希臘文已不再保有這種音，例如在 δνφός = 梵文 dhuma 等詞中。這個希臘文 θ 與 t 重疊成 τθήγ/υ，而梵文中的 dh 則與 d 重疊成 da-dhami。然而葛拉斯曼在其 1863 年發表的論文中證明，存在著某些詞根需要在詞首和詞尾都承認送氣音的存在，例如 DHIGH，在梵文中為 DIH；而在沒有濁音送氣音的希臘文中，這個詞根會出現為 τίξ 或 Giy 的形式，而不會是 St^ 或 dhiy。
為什麼會是如此，為什麼梵文中的媒介氣音（media aspirata）在失去氣音時，在希臘文中應由清音（tenuis）來表示，這很難確定地加以解釋。這可能是由於末尾氣音的反作用所致，在希臘文中氣音只能是清音；或者可能是因為初始氣音最初是清氣音（tenuis aspirata），因此其代用音也必然是清音。

可能曾經存在過，或者如某些人所言，必然存在過一個中間階段，在該階段初始媒介尚未成為清音（tenuis），也就是說，詞根在成為 tlx 之前可能是 Six，儘管在實際語言中對此毫無痕跡可尋。我們發現諸如 rolxos 和 Oiyydvco 這樣的衍生詞，但從未見過任何來自 hix 的衍生詞。這個 Otyyava），aor. ediyov 和 Oiyeiv 在音韻上完全正確，然而從前對詞語含義的尊重程度曾經是如此之高，以至於¹ ¹ 語言科學，第二卷，第 269 頁。
ASPIRATES, SONANT AND SURD. 403 葛拉斯曼本人實際上猶豫*不決，是否應將 Οὐσεῖν 溯源至詞根 DHIGH，單純因為意義上的差異。然而 digh 的原初意涵「揉捏」，可能恰如同 mm 的情況一樣，已被引申為更廣泛的意義如處理、觸及、思考。然而，語義學家有時也應當做出同樣的讓步，正如語音學家經常必須做出的讓步一樣。
神話性名稱皆為史前之物

希臘語經歷了一個不確定的時期，在於如何最恰當地表現梵文中出現的濁音送氣音 gh、dh、bh 的音值，我們從諸多例證中可以得知，在某些詞根中濁音與清音相互交替²。正是這樣一個事實：如 DHIGH 一類詞根的詞尾送氣音被濁音表現，如 diyeiv，而非清音（θῖκ），這表明其濁音的特徵仍然被感受到。因此，若這種獨有於過渡時期的濁音在此類詞根的詞尾得以規律地保存，那麼為何它從前³就不可能在詞首也被保存呢？
達芙妮。
若 dih 導向 toI-)(o<;，何以 Daphne 不能代表 Taphne，此乃音韻學上更為正確之形式？¹ ¹ 《比較神話學雜誌》（K. Z.），第十二卷，頁125。
2 關於 ἀTÓρισμοι 與 ἀ-τερρα 等，參閱《語言科學》第二卷，第 270 頁。
格拉斯曼論初始音位向清送氣音的轉變為一個歷時事件，見《語言比較雜誌》（K. Z.）第十二卷第117頁：「由於送氣音在梵語中是軟音，在希臘語中至少從某個時期起是硬音。」
我已準備好接收英文原文進行翻譯。 但我注意到您提供的「英文原文」欄位內容為： > D d 2 404 DAPHNE. [CHAP. 這看起來像是書籍頁碼和章節標記，而非完整的英文原文段落。 請提供完整的英文文本內容，我將按照您的規則忠實翻譯成繁體中文。

儘管在古典希臘語中已經絕跡？每當我們試圖發現神話名稱的詞源和原初意圖時，我認為首先必須做的是確定某些名稱所指涉的物理現象的類別。在達芙妮（Daphne）的例子中，我認為我已經證明了她在福埃波斯（Phoibos，燦爛的太陽）面前逃亡並在他的懷抱中消失的故事，可以與其他同源故事相對照，這些故事都指向曙光在初昇太陽燦爛光線觸及她時消失的現象。因此我們完全有權期待在達芙妮中發現一個曙光的名稱。然後，只有到了那時，才有了這個問題：曙光如何能被稱為達芙妮這樣的名稱？這樣的名稱有詞源，它是有目的而形成的，沒有真正的學者會否認這一點。因此，讓任何人提出一個比我多年前建議的更好的詞源——來自 DAH（dah, tax；φαίνειν, phaínein；Dacht）——如果確實如此，我自己的詞源當然必須被放棄。但不應遺忘的是，我的詞源不僅解釋了達芙妮作為曙光的名稱，而且同樣解釋了 daphne 作為月桂樹的名稱，即她據說被變成的那棵樹。我一直準備好放棄任何詞源，但前提是它能被更好的詞源所取代；但當我考慮到希臘語中清音塞音和濁音之間的易變性（Brugmann, Grundriss, par. 469, 8），當我看到實際的方言變化，如阿里斯托芬和色諾芬的語言中 δάπτις 對於 τα-π-ης 的變化時，我主張除非提出一個更好的詞源——更好不僅在音韻上，而且
v] 黛芙妮。405 在物質上也可以產生，我的理論仍然佔據優勢。

可以用更普遍的理由來說明為什麼在梵文中出現 d<f)i>7]，而不是 tol^vt]。格拉斯曼所證明的（*K. Z.*, xii, p. no）是：當某些根詞原本具有初聲和末聲送氣音時，在希臘語中出現時，初聲失去送氣成為清音，末聲則保留送氣。但如果某個詞是在雅利安語族分化之前形成的，該詞就不太可能受到專屬於希臘語的規則影響。現在大多數神話學名稱屬於所謂雅利安語言的史前或前民族層次，因此 Dahana 這樣的名稱會出現為 Daphana 或 Daphna，正如同 garbha 規律地出現為 fipefos，以及 SeX^u? 也出現為 Se\cf)6s 在 a-$e\<j)6$ 中一樣。garbha 的根是 gribh 或 gn'h（依據格拉斯曼的說法是 ghrabh）。以類似的方式，我們看到 /3v#/ao? 與 nvO/x-qp 並存，fiaTTTO) 與 gahate 並存，以及 fiadvs、fiados、/3ev0os，無論我們將它們與 gah（ghah）相連，或依據費克的說法與 bhadh 相連；我們有 So\t^o<? 與 dirgha 或 *daregha 並存，如此云云。
雅典娜。
從同一詞根 DAH，或更準確地說從其孿生形式 AH（如見於 ahan，即「日」），我在多年前曾嘗試推導雅典娜（Athene）之名，她是日女神、光明與智慧女神。提出了許多反對意見，我已試圖在《語言科學》第二卷第 621 頁起逐一作答。然而在此案例中，既然教授班費（Professor Benfey）曾提出另一套極具說服力的詞源學說，我便感到有責任加以闡明 406 雅典娜。[章
為什麼他的詞源推導在音韻上以及實質內容上都存在問題¹。每當在兩個詞源學說之間有所選擇時，就有必要說明我們基於何種根據來否定其中之一或另一個。

最近提出的一項反對意見並非新穎之舉，即若阿哈那（Ahana）與阿罕（ahan，日）相連，其 h 在希臘文中應要求 χ 而非 θ。然而我認為我在先前的場合已充分說明，關於梵文的濁送氣音（亦即希臘文的清送氣塞音），曾有一個時期，其清晰的痕跡仍保存在梵文中，當時 h 的位置尚未確定，可自由地表現為 gh、dh 或 bh。因此除了 nah 外，我們還發現 nabh（nabhi）和 nadh（naddha）。除了 grah 外，我們還發現 grabh 和 gradh（gṛdhra）。除了 gah（gacchati，深）外，我們還發現 gabh（gabhira，深）和 gadh（gadha，渡口）。在這類情況下，我們不再詢問最後的 h 或 gh 是軟齶音、硬齶音、唇音或齒音，而是將 gadh 這樣的詞根視為一個獨立的類型，並從中推導出 βαθύς（深），而將 βάθος（深度）從 gabh（gabh）推導出來。
